title: Data Center Copper Demand Supercycle
promotion_note: Promoted from On Our Radar to High Conviction on 2026-06-13 during age-out review. Original opp was 104 days old and the structural thesis has only firmed up since filing: AI capex commitments have grown (hyperscaler aggregate now ~$700B vs the $500B referenced at filing), the Hormuz disruption has added a fresh supply-side leg the original thesis did not contemplate, and FCX's Grasberg PB2/PB3 restart trajectory is still on schedule per the most recent reporting we have on file. The pattern is Secular Trend (no comparable closed opps in the ledger to flag, so no relaxed-bar penalty applies). Trade construction was not retrofitted per the first-run policy.
catalyst: AI data centers consume 5-10x more copper per megawatt than traditional facilities due to high-density power distribution, cooling systems, and electrical infrastructure. With $500B+ in planned data center capex globally through 2028, copper demand from this sector alone could absorb a significant portion of global mine supply growth.
thesis: The AI infrastructure buildout is creating structural demand for copper that the market hasn't fully priced in. Each GW of new data center capacity requires approximately 4,000-6,000 tons of copper for busbars, transformers, cabling, and cooling. Combined with electrification trends (EVs, grid upgrades, renewable connections), copper supply is expected to face a 5-10 million ton deficit by 2030. Producers with low-cost, long-life mines and expansion capacity are best positioned.

ticker_rationale.FCX: Freeport-McMoRan operates the Grasberg mine (world's largest copper/gold deposit) and has significant Americas-based production. With copper trading near all-time highs and Grasberg's underground ramp delivering higher grades, FCX offers direct leverage to the copper supercycle thesis. The company's U.S.-based smelter expansion also positions it for reshoring tailwinds.

risks: China economic slowdown could reduce near-term copper demand and depress prices
Copper substitution by aluminum in some data center applications could cap demand growth
Grasberg operational risks — underground mining in remote Papua, Indonesia
Tariff and trade war escalation could disrupt copper supply chains
summary: AI data centers consume 5-10x more copper per megawatt than traditional facilities, and the convergence of data center buildout, EV adoption, and grid electrification is creating a structural copper deficit that benefits the world's largest producer, Freeport-McMoRan.
body_md:
## Setup

Copper is the unsung commodity of the AI revolution. Every data center requires massive quantities of copper for busbars, transformers, power cables, cooling systems, and networking infrastructure — consuming 5-10x more copper per megawatt than traditional data center facilities [1]. With hyperscalers collectively committing $700 billion in AI infrastructure capex for 2026, the copper intensity of this buildout is creating demand growth that the mining industry cannot match with new supply for years.

S&P Global estimates that US AI data centers alone will generate approximately 110,000 tonnes of additional copper demand in 2026, with data center copper demand projected to reach 572,000 tonnes annually by 2028 [1]. But data centers are only one of three converging demand drivers. EV adoption requires 80 kg of copper per vehicle (vs. 23 kg for an ICE car), and grid electrification — including renewable energy connections and transmission upgrades — adds millions of tonnes of incremental demand. The International Copper Study Group projects a refined-copper deficit of roughly 150,000 tonnes in 2026, while UBS anticipates a larger deficit of 400,000 tonnes [1][2].

## Why Now

The timing is driven by the intersection of accelerating demand and constrained supply. On the demand side, Goldman Sachs estimates that AI will drive a 165% increase in data center power demand by 2030, with each GW of new capacity requiring 4,000-6,000 tonnes of copper [2]. This demand is additive to, not substitutive of, existing copper consumption from construction, automotive, and industrial sectors.

On the supply side, the pipeline of new copper mines is inadequate. Even if every currently announced copper project advances into production on schedule, the IEA projects a 30% supply gap by 2035 [1]. New copper mines take 10-15 years from discovery to production, meaning the supply response to current price signals won't arrive until the early 2030s. This structural imbalance supports copper prices above $5/lb for an extended period.

The geopolitical overlay adds further urgency. The Iran conflict and Hormuz blockade have disrupted global commodity logistics, and copper supply chains face additional risks from trade tensions, resource nationalism in Chile and Peru, and Indonesian regulatory uncertainty around Grasberg [3].

## Company-Specific Edge

Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) is the world's largest publicly traded copper producer, supplying approximately 8.5% of global mined copper from the massive Grasberg minerals district in Indonesia and significant Americas-based operations in Arizona, New Mexico, Colorado, Peru, and Chile [3].

The Grasberg underground transition — one of the most complex engineering projects in mining history — is reaching a critical inflection. The Block Cave mine restart is on track with PB2/PB3 targeting 85% production restoration by H2 2026, and full-year PTFI production is expected to approximate 1.0 billion pounds of copper and 0.9 million ounces of gold [3][4]. This production ramp, combined with elevated copper prices above $5/lb, drives the company's 2026 EBITDA guidance of approximately $12 billion (vs. $9.9 billion in 2025) [3].

Freeport's financial profile is strong for a cyclical producer. D/E of 34.1% reflects disciplined deleveraging, and operating cash flow of $5.6 billion in 2025 (with $8 billion projected for 2026) provides substantial reinvestment capacity [3]. The Americas Concentrator AI program — a partnership with McKinsey to deploy machine learning across processing operations — has achieved measurable improvements in copper recovery rates, effectively creating the output equivalent of a new processing facility without capital expenditure [5]. CEO Richard Adkerson's 20+ year tenure as the "elder statesman of copper" provides strategic continuity, and his public statements that "copper demand is driven by carbon reduction investments and AI" signal management's alignment with the secular thesis [5].

## Numbers That Matter

**Freeport-McMoRan (FCX)**:
- Copper production: 3.4 billion lbs (FY2025), targeting ~4B lbs run rate in 2026-2027
- Revenue: $25.9B (FY2025), with Q4 copper sales of 709M lbs at $5.33/lb average realized price
- EBITDA: ~$9.9B (FY2025), guided ~$12B (2026), $15.5B potential (2027-2028)
- Operating cash flow: $5.6B (FY2025), projected $8B (2026)
- Valuation: 16.1x forward P/E, D/E 34.1%, ROE 13.9%
- Capex: $4.3-4.5B annually (2026-2027) for capacity expansion
- Gold production: 1.1M ounces (FY2025) provides commodity diversification

## Bear Case

China remains the world's largest copper consumer (~55% of global demand), and a sustained economic slowdown or property sector contraction could depress near-term prices despite structural supply constraints. Copper prices have shown sensitivity to China PMI data and real estate starts, and any meaningful demand destruction from the world's largest consumer would overwhelm the data center demand thesis in the near term.

Copper substitution is a real but slow-moving risk. Aluminum is technically viable for some data center power distribution applications, and at sustained $5+/lb copper prices, engineering teams will increasingly design around copper where possible. This caps the upside on data center-specific demand growth, though substitution in wiring, motors, and transformers remains limited.

Grasberg operational risk deserves weight. Underground block-cave mining in remote Papua, Indonesia, carries geological, logistical, and political complexity. The Indonesian government holds 51% of PT Freeport Indonesia and has historically used regulatory leverage to extract concessions [3]. Any disruption to the Grasberg restart timeline would directly impact production guidance.

At 40x trailing P/E, the stock embeds meaningful recovery expectations. A return to $3.50/lb copper from current $5.30+ levels would slash EBITDA by 40%+ and render the growth capex program unsustainable.

## Conclusion

The copper data center demand thesis is a multi-year secular opportunity driven by the convergence of AI infrastructure buildout, EV adoption, and grid electrification against a structurally constrained supply pipeline. Freeport-McMoRan offers the most direct leverage as the world's largest public copper producer with a Grasberg production ramp that aligns with the demand inflection. At 16.1x forward P/E with projected EBITDA doubling from $9.9B to ~$15.5B by 2027-2028, the valuation is reasonable for a commodity producer entering a supercycle. The key monitoring points are copper prices (sustained above $5/lb supports the thesis), Grasberg PB2/PB3 restart progress, and China demand indicators.
invalidation: Copper prices fall below $4.00/lb for two consecutive months, signaling China demand destruction is overwhelming the structural supply deficit
Freeport-McMoRan Grasberg PB2/PB3 restart is delayed beyond Q4 2026, pushing back the production ramp that underpins 2027-2028 EBITDA targets
A major copper mine discovery or accelerated recycling technology reduces the projected 2030 supply deficit below 2 million tonnes
